test01.name: G1x04v
test01.description:
Here is the basic demographics of the people: The age is 22. The gender is male. A Indian people currently lives in Japan for 10 months. The education background is bachelor's in Technology and major in Aerospace Engineering. Currently is pursuing the master degree.

This individual tends to experience emotional reactions strongly and may be quick to feel nervous or unsettled. He prefers solitude and keeps to a smaller social circle. However, he is quite open-minded, inventive, and appreciates new experiences. He is considerate of others and values harmony. Also, he shows strong self-discipline and is goal-oriented. When it comes to crossing a street, he might overthink or hesitate, particularly in unpredictable traffic situations. Despite his thoughtful nature, he may need extra assurance from external cues or systems to make the decision to cross safely.
decision_criteria: Impression of autonomous driving: Very Positive. I have heard about Waymo cabs in the US, and also about Tesla's autonomous driving function.

Use case of autonomous driving: When I used to drive back home in India, I usually felt anxious and overwhelmed by the randomized and uncontrolled traffic scenarios. Drivers and bikers are usually all over the place, and if takes quite a toll on the drivers themselves. If this task may be automated, it might become safer, and I'd definitely embrace it in my daily life.
Emotion of autonomous driving: Neutral. They are still in their infancy, and there's still a lot of progress to be made. I am excited for what's in store for the future of autonomous vehicles.
Concern of autonomous driving: The current technology still doesn't seem mature enough. Maybe it'll take a few more years before we see them doing well on the roads.
Expectation of autonomous driving: Training the existing models a little more would probably help. The rate at which we're progressing, we'll probably get there soon.

test02.description:

This person maintains a remarkably calm and even-tempered attitude, rarely flustered by stress. Socially active and lively, he enjoys being around people and engaging in stimulating environments. He embraces novelty and is quick to explore new ideas. He is also empathetic and warm towards others. However, he tends to be less structured in his planning and might act on impulse. When deciding to cross a street, he is likely to act with confidence and spontaneity, relying on his social and situational awareness, even if the moment isn't perfectly planned out.
decision_criteria: Impression of autonomous driving: I love driving so much that I couldn't believe I would ever entrust it to an autonomous vehicle!
Use case of autonomous driving: When I need to park on the street, if it could automatically drive on the road, I wouldn't get ticketed lol.
Emotion of autonomous driving: Simply exciting.
Concern of autonomous driving: Frankly speaking, clarification of responsibility in the event of an accident occurring with Level 4/5 autonomous driving.
Expectation of autonomous driving: Expectations for safe driving on Japan's congested roads.

test07.name: G1x03v
test07.description:
Here is the basic demographics of the people: The age is 36. The gender is male. A Austria people currently lives in Japan for 5 years. Currently is pursuing the master degree.

Regarding the possible behavior when crossing a street: This person might act on emotion and heightened awareness of potential risks, making them likely to pause frequently or hesitate in unclear situations. However, their high energy and sociability may also make them willing to cross when others do, relying on collective momentum. They may seek novelty and alternative ways of crossing, or be more observant of signals or rules. Because they value cooperation and are generally dependable, they are likely to consider safety—both theirs and others’—before stepping into the street.
decision_criteria: Impression of autonomous driving: I have rode the autonomous shuttle bus. does that count? My impression is that there is a lot of money going into it, which may be spent better elsewhere.

Use case of autonomous driving: I think there needs to be a differentiation made within what the word 'vehicle' refers to. I cannot imagine ever using automated cars, because from my point of view, they are meant  to address an issue that could be much better addressed with proper public transport in 9 out of 10 cases. I have used automated busses and trams, and subways. As someone outside the vehicle, I would greatly prefer the latter two, as the rails make their paths predictable and safe.

Emotion of autonomous driving: Again, differentiating between cars and other forms of vehicles, autonomous cars, from my point of view, are a symptom of political neglect and indifference towards proper urban and transit planning, and seeing how much money gets poured into their development is quite vexing. I am not worried about the cars themselves, but rather, what their existence would mean for the future of urban planning. Even more neglect and indifference? In short, the ramifications for other, much more efficient modes of transport (walking, cycling, tram, train...) are more concerning. Automated trams, busses etc I would be less concerned about from a planning standpoint, but from a social point of view. Not just because it means a lot of people losing their jobs, but also because it could result in diminished public safety, as drivers also act as onboard security. In an automated bus, there would be nobody there to prevent someone from robbing the passengers and escaping, for example. So in conclusion, I feel like autonomous vehicles try to solve a problem that doesn't really exist.

Concern of autonomous driving: Again, differentiating between cars and other forms of vehicles, autonomous cars, from my point of view, are a symptom of political neglect and indifference towards proper urban and transit planning, and seeing how much money gets poured into their development is quite vexing. I am not worried about the cars themselves, but rather, what their existence would mean for the future of urban planning. Even more neglect and indifference? In short, the ramifications for other, much more efficient modes of transport (walking, cycling, tram, train...) are more concerning. Automated trams, busses etc I would be less concerned about from a planning standpoint, but from a social point of view. Not just because it means a lot of people losing their jobs, but also because it could result in diminished public safety, as drivers also act as onboard security. In an automated bus, there would be nobody there to prevent someone from robbing the passengers and escaping, for example. So in conclusion, I feel like autonomous vehicles try to solve a problem that doesn't really exist.
Expectation of autonomous driving: The actual use-case. As stated above, I think this is not a technical question, but a political and social question.

test08.name: G4x05g
test08.description:
Here is the basic demographics of the people: The age is 35. The gender is male. A Japanese people currently lives in Japan. Currently is pursuing the PhD.

Regarding the possible behavior when crossing a street: This person is likely to approach the crossing with steady awareness and confidence. Comfortable in social settings, they may follow group behavior if it aligns with logic. Their curiosity and flexibility could make them adaptable to both traditional and new traffic conditions. They are mindful of others, so would likely wait for the right moment to cross, taking shared safety into account. With a clear sense of structure and reliability, they’re inclined to follow traffic rules and respond well to organized systems like signals or crosswalks.
decision_criteria: Impression of autonomous driving: I have been in a Tesla car in the US (the Uber driver was there, but he wasn't steering). It felt strange that the steering wheel was automatically rotating, but I think it was just because I had never seen that before. I beleive that if everyone uses autonomous driving, overall traffic safety would increase.
Use case of autonomous driving: I can imagine it. If I live in suburb, I'd love to use it for everyday use: driving to an office, supermarkets or restaurants.
Emotion of autonomous driving: I think I will feel worried at first. After getting used to it, I think I will start feeling comfortable.

Concern of autonomous driving: I would feel more sleepy if I don't have to drive on my own. But people get sleepy even when they're driving and autonomous vehicles might be safer from that perspective.
Expectation of autonomous driving: No errors, Ability to deal with emergencies (e.g., people or animals running out into a street, a troublesome car tailgating me), Ability to wake drivers up

test09.name: G2x05g
test09.description:

Regarding the possible behavior when crossing a street: Likely to remain calm and make decisions without panic, this individual is socially confident and comfortable in active environments. While they enjoy exploring different options and scenarios, they prioritize their own judgment and goals over group behavior. Despite being task-oriented and goal-driven, they may not exhibit much concern for others' needs in the moment, unless it directly affects their path.
decision_criteria: Impression of autonomous driving: My first impression of autonomous driving is that it is an innovative and transformative technology.

Use case of autonomous driving: I can imagine using autonomous vehicles for daily commuting, especially in urban areas with heavy traffic. They could also be convenient for late-night rides or long-distance travel when I want to rest instead of driving.
Emotion of autonomous driving: Using an automated vehicle makes me feel curious and optimistic. I believe it could reduce stress from driving and improve safety.
Concern of autonomous driving: I sometimes worry about technical failures, cybersecurity, and how the vehicle would handle unexpected situations like sudden road hazards.

Expectation of autonomous driving: I hope automated vehicles will continue to improve in safety, reliability, and adaptability to various road conditions, while becoming more affordable and widely accessible.

decision_criteria: Impression of autonomous driving: I know the concept of autonomous driving, but I have never seen it in daily life. Since I can not drive a car, I look forward to a future where it is more integrated into our lives.

Emotion of autonomous driving: Although I have never used an automated vehicle, I feel curious rather than anxious. I find the technology fascinating, and I think it has great potential for real-world applications.
Concern of autonomous driving: One concern I have is the cost. I hope automated vehicles will be affordable enough for daily use.

Expectation of autonomous driving: I hope that automated vehicles will be designed to prioritize safety above all else — even if it means driving more slowly. My strong expectation is that they should never cause injury to people around them, under any circumstances.

decision_criteria: Impression of autonomous driving: I believe autonomous driving is the future, although I believe there still needs to be more development before it can be commonly accepted. I have traveled in autonomous bus and enjoyed the experience.
